expectations.text: Uses useWriteContracts hook (not useSendCalls or custom encoding)
expectations.evidence: Line 8 of page.tsx: `import { useCapabilities, useWriteContracts } from "wagmi/experimental";` and line 58: `const { writeContractsAsync, isPending: isBatchPending } = useWriteContracts();` — used correctly with contracts array containing approve+transfer calls.

expectations.text: Uses useCapabilities for wallet EIP-5792 support detection
expectations.evidence: Line 8 imports useCapabilities from wagmi/experimental. Lines 51-52: `const { isSuccess: isEIP5792Wallet, data: walletCapabilities } = useCapabilities({ account: connectedAddress });` and line 55 checks `walletCapabilities?.[chainId!]?.atomicBatch?.supported`.

expectations.text: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
expectations.evidence: No import or usage of useShowCallsStatus anywhere in the codebase. The page uses loading spinners and notifications for status feedback instead.

expectations.text: Provides graceful fallback for wallets without EIP-5792 support
expectations.evidence: Lines 122-152 implement `handleIndividualApproveAndTransfer` which performs approve then transfer as two separate transactions via `useScaffoldWriteContract`. Lines 246-269 conditionally render either the batch button or the fallback button based on `isBatchingSupported`. The fallback button text reads 'Approve + Transfer (2 txns)'.

expectations.text: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
expectations.evidence: Lines 246-269: When `isBatchingSupported` is false, the batch button is not rendered at all — instead, the fallback individual transaction button is shown. This is stronger than just disabling: the batch button is completely replaced with the fallback button. The batch button itself (line 250) is disabled when `isBatchPending || !recipient || !amount`.

expectations.text: ERC20 smart contract with approve+transfer pattern created
expectations.evidence: BatchToken.sol inherits from OpenZeppelin ERC20 which provides approve and transfer. The batch call in page.tsx (lines 97-110) calls approve then transfer on the BatchToken contract. The contract also has an open mint function for testing.

File `packages--hardhat--deploy--01_deploy_batch_token.ts` exists with proper hardhat-deploy pattern: uses `hre.getNamedAccounts()`, `hre.deployments.deploy()`, and sets `deployBatchToken.tags = ["BatchToken"]`.

expectations.text: Frontend page with batch UI created
expectations.evidence: File `packages--nextjs--app--batch-transfer--page.tsx` is a full 279-line page with token info card, mint card, and batch approve+transfer card. Header.tsx was modified to add navigation link to '/batch-transfer'.

expectations.text: Uses SE-2 scaffold hooks for contract interaction
expectations.evidence: Line 9 imports `useDeployedContractInfo, useScaffoldReadContract, useScaffoldWriteContract` from `~~/hooks/scaffold-eth`. Uses useScaffoldReadContract for balanceOf (line 22), name (line 28), symbol (line 33), and allowance (line 39). Uses useScaffoldWriteContract for mint and fallback approve/transfer (line 46). Uses useDeployedContractInfo to get contract ABI/address for batch calls (line 19).

expectations.text: No new npm dependencies needed (wagmi already has EIP-5792 hooks)
expectations.evidence: All EIP-5792 hooks (useWriteContracts, useCapabilities) are imported from 'wagmi/experimental' which is already part of the wagmi package. No package.json modifications were output. Summary confirms build passed without adding dependencies.

suggestions: Add useShowCallsStatus from wagmi/experimental to display the batch transaction status (pending/confirmed) in a more user-friendly way, rather than relying solely on loading spinners and notifications.
The implementation correctly uses useWriteContracts but misses the companion useShowCallsStatus hook which would provide wallet-native status tracking for the batch call.
eval_feedback.overall: Strong implementation that covers 9 of 10 expectations. The core EIP-5792 pattern is correctly implemented with useWriteContracts and useCapabilities, graceful fallback is well done, and SE-2 patterns are followed properly. The only gap is the missing useShowCallsStatus hook for batch transaction status display.
